Patch Set 3: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Patch Set 3: Looks good to me, approved

I'm thrilled to get rid of stuff like this.

Patch Set 3: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

I'm a little worried about this patch now. I was testing its drop_caches performance, and it looks like this might be making things worse for certain architectures.

My test was on a client with 16 processors and 64GB of ram. I would "dd" a varying number of ~4GB files, and then record the time it takes to "echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches". I tested 2.3.58-7chaos vs 2.3.58-8chaos, the only difference is "8chaos" had patch set 2 of this change applied.

Here are the results:

    2.3.58-7chaos                                                               
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
    | # files | Start kB | End kB |    real   |   user   |    sys    |          
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
    |    1    |  4238572 |  44000 |  0m1.700s | 0m0.000s |  0m1.694s |          
    |    2    |  8436280 |  43964 |  0m3.790s | 0m0.000s |  0m3.776s |          
    |    4    | 16821488 |  57316 |  0m6.991s | 0m0.000s |  0m6.967s |          
    |    8    | 33611928 |  44224 | 0m14.740s | 0m0.000s | 0m14.689s |          
    |   16    | 27359148 |  44624 | 0m12.011s | 0m0.000s | 0m11.964s |          
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
                                                                                
    2.3.58-8chaos                                                               
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
    | # files | Start kB | End kB |    real   |   user   |    sys    |          
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
    |    1    |  4260804 |  44196 |  0m4.286s | 0m0.000s |  0m4.274s |          
    |    2    |  8433148 |  44120 |  0m8.486s | 0m0.000s |  0m8.464s |          
    |    4    | 16825160 |  44668 | 0m17.129s | 0m0.000s | 0m17.084s |             
    |    8    | 33598976 |  44492 | 0m35.772s | 0m0.000s | 0m35.687s |             
    |   16    | 49387408 |  44212 | 0m51.160s | 0m0.000s | 0m51.038s |             
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+

The "Start kB" and "End kB" are the values of the "Cached:" field in /proc/meminfo. The "real", "user", and "sys" are times as reported by the "time" command. The test was essentially what was used in LU-1784.

As you can see, it appears patch set 2 makes the drop_caches time take *twice* as long to complete on this particular machine. Not good. More investigation is needed.

Patch Set 3:

Do you have a chance to work out a percpu env cache(one preallocated env for each CPU) for ll_releasepage()? This implies preemption will be disabled during the time when the env is being used in ll_releasepage(). This should solve the drop_cache problem and the extra benefit will be no longer to allocate memory to free a page.

Patch Set 3:

So you're suggesting we keep around a single cl_env for each CPU which is solely used for calls to ll_releasepage? And keep preemption disabled the entire time ll_releasepage is running?

Just want to make sure I understand correctly. I can work up a patch to do that and try it out tomorrow.

Patch Set 3:

exactly.

Patch Set 3:

It would be nice if we could just remove the need for a cl_env in ll_releasepage altogether. I'm unsure how much work that would be though..

Patch Set 3:

I pushed an initial version of the per cpu cl_env for ll_releasepage patch here: http://review.whamcloud.com/5446

It's not ready for landing yet, but is there for comments.

Patch Set 3:

OK, so I tested with patch set 2 of http://review.whamcloud.com/5446, and the drop_caches performance appears to be roughly what it was prior to this patch.

Here's the numbers:
    2.3.58-13chaos3surya1 (essentially 2.3.58-8chaos + 5446 ps2)                
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
    | # files | Start kB | End kB |    real   |   user   |    sys    |          
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+          
    |    1    |  4239380 |  45108 |  0m1.827s | 0m0.000s |  0m1.820s |          
    |    2    |  8433684 |  45100 |  0m3.697s | 0m0.000s |  0m3.684s |          
    |    4    | 16832240 |  44656 |  0m7.509s | 0m0.000s |  0m7.483s |          
    |    8    | 24698280 |  45008 | 0m11.135s | 0m0.000s | 0m11.082s |          
    |   16    | 25391416 |  45484 | 0m11.892s | 0m0.000s | 0m11.840s |          
    +---------+----------+--------+-----------+----------+-----------+ 

The only "wierd" thing I noticed is the cached bytes (i.e. Start kB) did not grow as large in this test as the others, when the files and number of bytes read was the same.

Should I pull http://review.whamcloud.com/5446 into this patch set? That way the regression and fix are in the same patch. There could also be case for keeping them separate since they're independent and isolated changes.
Uploaded patch set 4.

Patch Set 4:

I pulled the ll_releasepage optimization into this patch.

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.3/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/42bd3b76-7b12-11e2-8242-52540035b04c).  Ran 12 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 5: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Triggered this when running on a Grove client, with this patch applied:

    LustreError: 8145:0:(cl_object.c:755:cl_env_new()) ASSERTION( (!(CFS_ALLOC_IO != CFS_ALLOC_ATOMIC) || (!(((current_thread_info()->preempt_count) & ((((1UL << (10))-1) << ((0 + 8) + 8)) | (((1UL << (8))-1) << (0 + 8)) | (((1UL << (1))-1) << (((0 + 8) + 8) + 10))))))) ) failed:

I think this assertion failed in __OBD_SLAB_ALLOC_VERBOSE:

    LASSERT(ergo(type != CFS_ALLOC_ATOMIC, !cfs_in_interrupt()));

Patch Set 5: No score

After more testing, I'm not convinced this patch is to blame for the crash I saw earlier.

Patch Set 5: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

I think this is a good change.  In general the global list did not look to to have a reclaim method for situations with memory pressure and that does not seem good as well.  Using the slab cache more directly seems to be a good idea to me.

Patch Set 5:

Right, previously there were no reclaim hooks for this list. Keep in mind, I did experience about a 2x increase in the time it took to drop caches on my test system with this change. Which is a regression, IMO, but http://review.whamcloud.com/5446 was introduced to fix that.
Patch Set 5: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Patch Set 5:

Hi Prakash, can you please remind me where is the patch to add percpu env array for ll_releasepage()?

Patch Set 5:

Jinshan, I added you as a reviewer of that patch. Also, here's a link to it: http://review.whamcloud.com/5446

Patch Set 5: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

I temporarily deny this patch to -1 because it can't pass performance benchmark. I'm looking for the root cause.

Patch Set 5: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

This patch is okay to go.
Patch Set 5: Looks good to me, approved

parents.subject: LU-1187 tests: add cleanup for sanity 24q.

commit.subject: LU-2622 obdclass: Remove the global cl_env list
commit.message:
LU-2622 obdclass: Remove the global cl_env list

New cl_env structures are allocated using a SLAB cache specifically
created for allocating and freeing these structure. Without this patch,
when a thread is finished with a cl_env structure it places it on a
global list instead of freeing it back to the SLAB cache. With this
patch, this global list is completely removed, and cl_env structures are
released immediately back to the SLAB cache.

The motivation for this change essentially boils down to this secondary
global list cache being completely unnecessary, and only serving to
serialize any calls to cl_env_get and cl_env_put. This has proven to
cause a severe performance impact on large core count systems,
specifically during memory reclamation (i.e. ll_releasepage).

For example, on BG/Q Sequoia IO nodes, we've experienced nearly all
68 cores of a machine spinning on the lock protecting this global
list. Some example stack traces showcasing this problem were gathered
using sysrq-l, and are displayed below:

    CPU56:
    Call Trace:
    [c00000000fe3bb30] [c000000000008d1c] .show_stack+0x7c/0x184 (unreliable)
    [c00000000fe3bbe0] [c00000000027604c] .showacpu+0x64/0x94
    [c00000000fe3bc70] [c000000000068b30] .generic_smp_call_function_interrupt+0x10c/0x230
    [c00000000fe3bd40] [c00000000001d11c] .smp_message_recv+0x34/0x78
    [c00000000fe3bdc0] [c00000000002526c] .bgq_ipi_dispatch+0x118/0x18c
    [c00000000fe3be50] [c00000000007b20c] .handle_IRQ_event+0x88/0x18c
    [c00000000fe3bf00] [c00000000007dc90] .handle_percpu_irq+0x8c/0x100
    [c00000000fe3bf90] [c00000000001b808] .call_handle_irq+0x1c/0x2c
    [c0000003e1c4a4c0] [c0000000000059f0] .do_IRQ+0x154/0x1e0
    [c0000003e1c4a570] [c0000000000144dc] exc_external_input_book3e+0x110/0x114
    --- Exception: 501 at ._raw_spin_lock+0xd8/0x1a8
        LR = ._raw_spin_lock+0x104/0x1a8
    [c0000003e1c4a860] [8000000000b04f38] libcfs_nidstrings+0x2acc/0xfffffffffffe5824 [libcfs] (unreliable)
    [c0000003e1c4a910] [c00000000042d4cc] ._spin_lock+0x10/0x24

    [c0000003e1c4b450] [8000000006920964] .ll_write_begin+0x94/0x270 [lustre]
    [c0000003e1c4b520] [c0000000000968c8] .generic_file_buffered_write+0x148/0x374
